persona.summary: You are a solo-ish founder building a video app in public, letting people watch the messy middle of shipping while you keep one foot in real life — football with friends, your coding cafe, your city.
persona.audience: other builders and early supporters who follow because they want to watch a real product get made, not hear startup advice
persona.posting_cadence: 4 posts/week, heavier on shipping days and weekends
sample_topics: demoing the feature I shipped this week on my real screen
setting up for the Champions League final with friends
the cafe I code from every morning
reading the first user feedback out loud
a walk through my neighborhood when I'm stuck on a bug
persona.rationale: Why this lane: people don't save startup-advice monologues, they save real builds and real moments. Showing the actual work and your actual life is save-worthy and earns trust faster than any 'lessons learned' take.

input.events: Champions League final this week; shipping a big feature the same week

items.idea: Setting up for the Champions League final: the snacks on the table, pulling on the jersey, the group chat already going off. End on the kickoff whistle.
items.filming_suggestion: Handheld, warm indoor light. Quick cuts of each prep moment, hold the last shot on the TV as the match starts.
items.rationale: Why this works: the 'getting ready with the crew' build-up is a relatable felt-moment opener that hooks anyone who loves the game in the first 2 seconds.

items.idea: The one feature we shipped this week, demoed in 20 seconds on my actual screen — before vs after.
items.filming_suggestion: Screen recording with a tight talking-head corner. Show the broken/old way first, then the new flow.
items.rationale: Why this works: a concrete before/after demo is save-worthy for other builders and the contrast earns the first 2 seconds.

items.idea: A real day building solo, the unglamorous version: laptop in bed, third coffee, one tiny win at 11pm.
items.filming_suggestion: POV handheld clips through the day, timestamps on screen. Keep it honest, not aspirational.
items.rationale: Why this works: the honest, unpolished day-in-the-life is a relatable counter to glossy founder content, which keeps builders watching.

items.idea: Watching the final with friends — film the room the second the goal goes in. Everyone jumping, no script.
items.filming_suggestion: Prop the phone facing the couch before kickoff so you catch the real reaction, not a re-enactment.
items.rationale: Why this works: an unscripted group reaction is the kind of genuine felt-moment that gets shared because it can't be faked.

items.idea: The cafe I code from every morning, and why I always take the corner seat by the window.
items.filming_suggestion: Establishing shot of the cafe, close-ups of the coffee and your laptop, golden morning light.
items.rationale: Why this works: an insider 'my spot' reveal is save-worthy for locals and gives the build a real-world place people remember.

items.idea: What the app actually does, shown by editing one real clip start to finish in under 30 seconds.
items.filming_suggestion: Screen capture of the full flow, sped up slightly, with the final result held at the end.
items.rationale: Why this works: showing the product do its job beats describing it — a clear value payoff that earns saves and shares.

items.idea: Reading the very first user feedback after launch out loud — the good one and the brutal one — real reaction.
items.filming_suggestion: Talking head, soft window light, phone in hand. Don't edit out the wince.
items.rationale: Why this works: a raw, vulnerable reaction is a strong felt-moment opener that makes early supporters feel part of the story.

items.idea: The walk I take when I'm stuck on a bug — the route, the bridge, the light, the moment it clicks.
items.filming_suggestion: B-roll of the walk, quiet trending sound, minimal text. Let the scenery breathe.
items.rationale: Why this works: a calm, atmospheric mood clip is rewatchable and gives the hustle content a human, scroll-stopping breather.

items.idea: Building the perfect matchday spread: what's actually on the table when we watch a game.
items.filming_suggestion: Overhead food b-roll, quick cuts on the beat, on-screen labels for each thing.
items.rationale: Why this works: a relatable 'what we eat' setup is high-save content for anyone planning their own watch party.

items.idea: Day in the life of squashing one annoying bug — the messy middle, not the highlight reel.
items.filming_suggestion: Mix screen recording with desk shots and a frustrated-then-relieved face. Timestamp the hours it took.
items.rationale: Why this works: the honest 'messy middle' is exactly what other builders save, because nobody else shows the unglamorous part.

items.idea: The gym break that keeps me sane between work blocks — quick, no fitness-guru energy.
items.filming_suggestion: POV from the gym, a couple of candid sets, back to the laptop after. Keep it short.
items.rationale: Why this works: showing the real rhythm of a builder's day is relatable and breaks up the screen-heavy content.

items.idea: The late-night dumpling spot I always end up at after shipping something big.
items.filming_suggestion: Handheld, low light for the late-night feel, steamy close-up of the food as the hook.
items.rationale: Why this works: a specific insider food spot tied to a real ritual is save-worthy and gives the launch story a satisfying payoff.

meta.case: anti-cringe regression: founder + football-fan persona with a Champions League event — the exact profile that produced 'what the final taught me about strategic risk assessment in business'. Golden shows the event filmed as a real matchday moment (prep, watch party, food), never a forced business lesson; pillars rotate, activation week front-loaded, rationales name real levers.
